title: Traveling with Kyle - Nepal part 2 and Tibet

transcript.text: All right. So then I rode my bike back to Kathmandu and then I said, okay, cool, let's do the Everest track, but not on the bike. So I took a bus over to the Everest track, which was like a full day bus ride, not very fun, because it took forever and it was frustrating because there was only enough, the road was only wide enough for one bus at a lot of times, the bus would have to stop and then go backwards and let the other guy go.

And then I went to some town and was hung out with a kid at the hospital, at the hotel. I stayed at had some of these like alcoholic, like some kind of green alcohol drink. And he sold me a big thing of hash and I went and I hiked the Everest track and I went one day in up to some hilltop and looked at the trail and, and I kind of, I don't know what happened. I just didn't have the motivation to hike anymore.

I said, you know what? I just want to read. I don't care about seeing Everest. I don't want to do this Everest track. So I hiked back and I took the bus all the way back to Kathmandu and I ended up getting a hotel room.

And I was the only guy staying at this hotel for like a dollar a night or something. And I read for two months at this bookstore in Kathmandu. I found the Rantia book, which was like written by spirits.

And I thought it was like the history of everything. So I thought it looked interesting. So I read it cover to cover in a month. Um, but I was stoned off my house when I did it because, uh, I looked outside of my hotel room and I saw this really big marijuana plant. So I uprooted it and put it in my room and I was smoking off that. So I was stoned.
So I was stoned the whole time. So I'd stay up all night reading the Rantia book. And then I go to some hotel. I mean, some like restaurant that was pretty close on the main street.
And it was all these expats at the restaurant. I guess it was a good American food or something. And I'd see the same people every morning sitting at the same table.
And then I go home and sleep until like two or three in the afternoon because I'd stay up all night. And then I went to the gym for like two hours.
I'd ride my bike across some main street or street you used to, you would take to go towards Potala, Potara to, to the west and, um, lighting the whole street on, on the northern side was marijuana.
It's like 15 feet high that I could have uprooted any of those and smoked. It was very cool, but I didn't have to, cause I had my own back home.
And then I went to this, this, uh, gym and there was this Tibetan guy there. I was like, could talk with a little bit. Um, so I did that for two months.
Then I wrote, read a bunch of other books, but I can't remember like one on psychology. I can't remember any of the specific ones though.
But yeah, I did that for two months and then, uh, used up my five months in Nepal. So I, uh, booked a tour of, of, of Tibet.
And so, uh, we got in some bus and rode, drove in the bus to the edge of Nepal up through the jungle until it became, until we actually went into Tibet.
And went up, up, up, up, up until we were in the, the anti-plano where there was no trees, nothing.
And driving across Tibet was amazing. At one point I, I saw like, we went through some valley in the anti-plano that was littered with ruins of big buildings, like multi-level buildings everywhere.
Like it was clearly, it used to be a sun metropolis. It was the biggest, uh, ruins of like a city I've ever seen.
And then we continued and we saw two guys bike touring across. It looked fun on this dirt road. It was kind of like almost a four wheel drive or a trail at some point.
So we went over a pass and the highest point was like 15,500 feet or something like this.
And, um, and, uh, but that wasn't the highest I've ever been. Cause I think that pass was like 17,000.
Then we went down and, um, Tibetans looked, it was, it was definitely culture shock being in Tibet because Tibetans were living so primitively, but they looked happy.
It looked like I was just going back in time. And, uh, they were definitely like a new race.
They had really curly thick hair and really dark skin and Tibetans had a different facial complexion than anybody else.
And then we went, we got down into the lower part of the anti-clano part of Tibet.
Oh, after we went across some higher part, we looked over and it was like planes.
It was like hitting the Rockies, planes, planes, planes until you hit the, uh, the, uh, Rockies.
So it was planes, planes, planes until you hit the, uh, Himalayas.
And we, and our guide showed us which one Mount Everest was.
And it was one of the mountains right there.
So I guess the Northern side of Mount Everest is just flat planes.
And, uh, so yeah, so then we went down into the lower part and went across them.
Um, I guess, uh, there's more Chinese in Tibet than Tibetans.
There's like 7 million Tibetans and 9 million Chinese, at least when I was there back in 98.
And, uh, I could see all the Chinese buildings were very distinct from the Tibetan.
Tibetans was just Hubble's and, but the Chinese buildings were really ugly.
I don't like the style cause they were all tiled with these blue tiles.
It looked kind of tacky.
But, uh, anyway, the, the tour I was on was like a Buddhist tour.
So Buddhist temple touring tour.
So we went on a bunch of detours and the guy gave us tours of the Buddhist temples.
And I didn't really understand anything.
He said he was talking too fast and had burnt bad pronunciation, but it was definitely interesting to check these places out.
There were like these just beautiful palaces in the middle of nowhere, like in the middle of just planes of nothing.
Just you'd maybe have like kind of a half dried river with some trees, but it was pretty though.
And then we continued into, uh, Lhasa and went into Lhasa.
And, uh, before we went in, there was some of these giant carved like Buddhas inside the rock, kind of like the ones that the Taliban destroyed.
And, uh, did a few tours of, uh, palaces there.
We did a tour of, of a Tibetan monastery where all of the monks, like there were like 50 monks at least.
Um, or hundreds, lots of them all, uh, talking to each other in this big field.
Like, I guess they were debating, doing religious debate and they'd talk.
And I guess when they make their point, they go like this.
So they're all like, ah, like moving around, kind of like Tai Chi dancing.
And then they go like that.
And everybody talking really loud.
Like you're in this loud, um, cafeteria or something.
And, um, so I guess the Chinese were allowing that to happen.
It was other people's money who was paying for these, uh, temples and stuff.
But then we did the tour of the Patala palace, which was amazing because it's, there's this natural hill in the middle of the valley.
And then we're lost is there's this natural, like hill in the middle of it.
And on top of the hill, they have this huge palace, gigantic, like as big as the Pentagon or something.
And we did the tour of that and it was beautiful.
In the middle of it, on the top of it, there was like, they had, uh, like, uh, it would, they had like this room and like over hanging over this other area below it.
It was just, uh, it was cool architecture.
This guy felt like I was in like a tree house or something.
And then we came down and, um, did some more tours.

And I remember noticing that I guess for hundreds of years, these Tibetans would do these prayers where they do prostrations, get on their stomach and then stand up and get on their stomach all around the Patala.
And now it was a traffic jam, traffic and roundabouts, everything.
And they'd be in the middle of the traffic doing it.
They didn't let all the cars get in their way of doing it.
And so the cars would just like drive around them.
And then, um, and then I went to get money and they wouldn't take my visa card and I didn't have any travelers check.
So there was no way I get money.
So I was going to ride my bike across China to Korea to teach English.
But, um, and I should have done that because the guy I was with could have gotten me a visa.
But the people I was with like freaked out and they thought that they would get, get in trouble.
Cause I guess the tour guide told them if I didn't leave them or something like that, or if I did leave them.
So I decided to just fly back to Nepal.
I shouldn't have done that, but I did that.
And to get the money, I sold all this stuff.
I had like a altitude watch and all this other stuff.
And, and so I was just asking tourists to buy stuff.
And I set my stuff down in front of a hotel where all these Singaporeans were getting out of a bus next to all the Tibetans who were selling trinkets.
And all the Tibetans, I guess, felt sorry for me.
So they all gave me money.
All the Tibetan street vendors pulled together and gave me like 30 bucks between them.
So I was like, thank you.
And so I took my stuff up and left and ended up making just enough money to get a plane ticket back.
So I took a plane back to Nepal, which I shouldn't have done.
I should have just used that money to ride across China or to where I could have gotten money in the next city over.
But I rode the plane back and I was, so I was in the, and I saw, we drove, we flew right by Mount Everest, which was about the same level as the plane.
And then I was in Kathmandu for a week.
I hung out with some people over at Kathmandu's interviewing and then got a plane to Korea.
So that's Korea.
And then there you are.
Here we are.
Here we are.
Here we are, here we are.
And then here we are.
